title: 2025 Community Survey Results
intro_paragraphs: The 2025 NixOS Community Survey ran from September 1 to December 15, 2025, drawing 3,399 full responses — a 48% increase over the 2,290 received in 2024.

The growth reflects a noticeably newer audience: 28.9% of respondents started using Nix in the past year, and 82.5% have under four years of experience. New questions in this year's survey, and the additional charts that accompany them, were added to surface insights into who that new majority is, what tools they use, and where they look for help.

sections.heading: People
sections.note: Categories with fewer than 5 respondents are not shown.

rows.title: Country
rows.question: Where do you live?
rows.commentary:
European responses climbed to 60.0%, up from 52% a year earlier, while
North America slipped from 27.5% to 21.3%. The remaining regions each
moved by less than a point.

rows.title: Age
rows.question: What is your age in years?
rows.commentary:
The age curve flattened. The 25-34 cohort, still the largest, fell from
35.6% to 30.7%, and 35-44 dropped from 22.7% to 19.4%. Every group
under 25 or over 44 grew, by between 0.4 and 3.1 points — a modest but
broad-based widening.

rows.title: Gender Identity
rows.question: What is your gender identity?
rows.commentary:
The community is overwhelmingly male: 78.3% of respondents identify
as men. Women account for 7.2% and non-binary respondents 5.9%; a
further 6.6% declined to answer.

rows.title: Transgender Identity
rows.question: Do you identify as transgender?
rows.commentary:
A majority (81.8%) do not identify as transgender; 9.8% do, and 7.2%
declined to answer.

rows.title: Years Coding
rows.question: How many years of programming experience do you have (including time spent learning or studying)?
rows.commentary:
Reported programming experience leans long: most respondents fall in
the 1–14 year bands. Groups with four or fewer years of experience
grew compared with the prior year, while groups with five or more
years shrank. This year's instrument also splits the 10+ bracket into
finer bins, surfacing more detail at the experienced end.

rows.title: Role
rows.question: Which of the following best describes your primary role or occupation?
rows.commentary:
Students lead at 24.0%, followed by full-stack (13.4%) and back-end
developers (10.7%); system and network administrators trail at 4.8%.
A further 10.3% selected "Other".

rows.title: Industry
rows.question: What best describes your economic sector?
rows.commentary:
"Other" is the largest single category at 21.0%, suggesting the listed
industries miss common workplaces. Academia or education (17.6%) is the
largest named category. Telecom (5.9%), Consulting (5.8%), and Financial
services (4.7%) follow.

data: Machine learning or AI model development

rows.title: Domain
rows.question: What best describes your software domain?
rows.commentary:
"Other" leads at 15.7%, suggesting the listed domains miss common
categories. Web development (13.8%), open-source software development
(10.8%), and programming languages / compilers / code analysis (9.2%)
follow.

data: Cloud / PaaS operations (e.g. provisioning, monitoring, SRE)
Operational or end-user software tooling (e.g. low-code platforms)
Cloud / PaaS development (e.g. infrastructure tooling, APIs)
Programming languages, compilers, or code analysis tooling

rows.title: Operating Systems
rows.question: Which operating systems do you regularly use on your devices (e.g. for development, work, or personal use)?
rows.commentary:
Nearly all respondents — 96.9% — use Linux on at least one device. Android
(57.8%) and Windows (35.5%) are next, with macOS at 24.4% and iOS at 19.6%.
Categories below 0.5% or with fewer than 5 respondents are combined into the
"Other (combined)" bar.

rows.title: Operating systems running Nix
rows.question: On which operating systems do you currently use Nix?
rows.commentary:
Among respondents who use Nix, NixOS itself is the primary host
(90.0%). Other GNU/Linux distributions (26.5%), macOS (18.1%), and
Windows via WSL (11.0%) round out the meaningful platforms. 3.7%
report not using Nix at all.

rows.title: Target Triple
rows.question: Which target triple best describes your system? Run "uname -a" if you are not sure.
rows.commentary:
`x86_64-linux-gnu`: 94.5%. ARM combined (`aarch64-linux-gnu` 17.4% +
`aarch64-apple-darwin` 16.2%) reaches 33.6%. RISC-V: 2.0%.

rows.title: Experimental Features
rows.question: Which Nix experimental features do you use?
rows.commentary:
Flakes (78.9%) and the new `nix` CLI (66.7%) lead. The next-ranked feature
(pipe operators, 8.0%) trails by 58.7 points. 10.9% report using no
experimental features; 7.9% are not sure.

data: Allow forcing trust settings for the Nix daemon (`daemon-trust-override`)
Allow derivation builders to call Nix (`recursive-nix`)
Allow impure environment variables in the execution environment (`configurable-impure-env`)
I don’t use any experimental features

rows.title: Install Method
rows.question: How have you installed Nix on any of your systems?
rows.commentary:
Most respondents (78.4%) first met Nix through a NixOS installation.
The official install script accounts for another 36.0%, followed by
the `DeterminateSystems/nix-installer` at 15.8%. Self-built and
third-party-packaged installations together cover the remaining 13.6%.

data: I used another package manager (e.g. Homebrew, APT)
I used the official install script from nixos.org

rows.title: Nix Implementations
rows.question: Which of these do you use?
rows.commentary:
Upstream Nix remains the default by a wide margin (87.5%). Lix (14.8%)
and Determinate Nix (9.8%) are the most-used alternatives. Tvix (0.8%)
and Snix (1.2%) are still in single digits.

rows.title: Infrastructure
rows.question: On what types of systems or infrastructure do you use Nix?
rows.commentary:
Personal computers (90.3%) are the dominant target. Workstations
(55.8%) and home servers (52.6%) follow. Production servers (21.4%)
and CI environments (20.5%) are used at roughly a quarter the rate
of personal machines.

rows.title: Nix Version
rows.question: Which version of Nix do you use most often? (Enter the full SemVer version, e.g. 2.18.5. You can check by running `nix-env --version`.)
rows.commentary:
The 2.28 series dominates the answers that could be parsed: 2.28.5
alone accounts for 21.7%, with 2.28.4 at 10.5%. Roughly a third of
respondents skipped the question; a further 6.4% gave an unparseable
answer.

Versions are extracted with a SemVer regex against the free-text
response. Unparseable answers are labelled "No Match" and bins below
0.1% or 5 respondents are folded into "Other (combined)".

rows.title: NixOS Release
rows.question: Which NixOS releases do you currently use on any of your systems?
rows.commentary:
25.05 (the current stable) leads at 61.6%, followed closely by
unstable at 56.8%. The two combined exceed 100%, indicating many
respondents run more than one release across their machines. Older
releases drop off sharply: 24.11 at 6.6%, 24.05 at 2.2%, and 23.11 or
earlier at 1.4%.

rows.title: Hardware Configuration
rows.question: How do you configure hardware on NixOS?
rows.commentary:
`nixos-generate-config` is still the dominant entry point at 68.6%,
with manual configuration cited by 39.0% and the `nixos-hardware`
repository by 26.7%. `nixos-facter` lands at 3.9%.

rows.title: Software Ecosystems
rows.question: Which software ecosystems do you use Nix with?
rows.commentary:
NixOS configurations leads at 81.1% (a meta-result — Nix users using Nix for
their own systems). Among programming languages: Python 45.8%, Rust 42.9%,
Bash 36.8%, C 26.7%, JavaScript 23.5%, C++ 22.7%, Go 21.3%.
Categories below 0.5% or with fewer than 5 respondents are combined into the "Other (combined)" bar.

rows.title: Stable Upgrade
rows.question: If you use NixOS stable, how was your experience upgrading to the new stable version (25.05)?
rows.commentary:
Among respondents who answered, most had a clean upgrade: 45.4% report
no issues and 23.7% only minor ones, while moderate, severe-resolved,
and severe-unresolved issues together account for 8.6%. A further 15.5%
had not yet upgraded at survey time, and 6.7% were unaware a new
stable release existed.

The Sankey funnel below re-stages these responses as a flow — from all
respondents, to whether they knew a new stable release existed, to whether
they upgraded, to the severity they hit. This staging is a derived analyst
interpretation, not a survey field: the single question cannot truly separate
"knew but chose not to upgrade" from other reasons for staying put, so the
funnel is exploratory and meant to be read visually rather than as exact
sub-populations. The companion Sankey links experience (years using Nix) to
the upgrade outcome.

series.name: I had severe issues but figured it out after some time.
I had severe issues and could not make the upgrade.
I did not know there was a new stable release.

key.label: I had severe issues but figured it out after some time.
I had severe issues and could not make the upgrade.
I did not know there was a new stable release.

rows.title: Involvement
rows.question: Which of these best describe your involvement with the Nix ecosystem?
rows.commentary:
Nearly every respondent uses NixOS (87.1%), and two-thirds (66.7%)
install software with Nix more broadly. Roughly half maintain NixOS
machines (58.1%) or develop software on Nix (48.8%). Direct
contribution to Nixpkgs is much narrower: 22.8% have submitted
packages or patches, 15.5% maintain packages, and 2.9% hold merge
access.

data: I have merge access to Nixpkgs
I develop tools or infrastructure that use or integrate with Nix, Nixpkgs, or NixOS
I contribute packages or patches to Nixpkgs
I manage or maintain machines running NixOS
I use Nix to install software

rows.title: Years using Nix
rows.question: How many years have you been using Nix (at any level of involvement)?
rows.commentary:
The community skews new: 28.9% have used Nix for less than a year,
34.0% for one to two years, and 19.6% for three to four. Combined,
82.5% of respondents have under four years of Nix experience. Only
12.3% have used Nix for five or more years.

rows.title: Nix Skill Level
rows.question: How would you describe your skill level with Nix?
rows.commentary:
Self-described skill remains roughly stable year over year:
Intermediate (45.6%) is the largest group, followed by Beginner
(38.9%) and Advanced (10.2%).

rows.title: First heard of: Nix or NixOS
rows.question: Did you hear about Nix or NixOS first? If you heard about both at the same time, select which one first made you interested in the Nix ecosystem.
rows.commentary:
NixOS is the more common entry point into the ecosystem: 70.8% of
respondents heard about it first, against 20.0% who heard of Nix
first. 7.6% no longer remember.

rows.title: First-exposure channel
rows.question: How did you first hear about Nix or NixOS?
rows.commentary:
YouTube is the single largest channel (28.3%), well ahead of
word-of-mouth from a friend or colleague (15.9%). 12.0% no longer
remember. Blogs (7.3%), search (6.7%), Reddit (4.9%), and work
introductions (4.6%) make up the next tier. Categories below 0.5% or
fewer than 5 respondents are folded into "Other (combined)".

data: In academic research or a conference talk
On a blog or personal website

rows.title: Discovery flow
rows.question:
Did you hear about Nix or NixOS first? If you heard about both at the same time, select which one first made you interested in the Nix ecosystem.
 → How did you first hear about Nix or NixOS?
rows.commentary:
This alluvial view links whether respondents first heard of Nix or NixOS
(left) to the channel that introduced them (right). It re-expresses the two
discovery questions as a single flow: NixOS-first respondents dominate every
channel, and YouTube and word-of-mouth carry the heaviest streams. Channels
reaching fewer than 5 respondents in a given flow are dropped, so only the
substantial paths are drawn.

data.name: In academic research or a conference talk
On a blog or personal website

links.target: In academic research or a conference talk
On a blog or personal website
On a blog or personal website
In academic research or a conference talk
On a blog or personal website

rows.title: User types
rows.question: Which user types do you identify with?
rows.commentary:
"Love the idea" (A) is by far the most-claimed identity, picked by
85.3% of respondents. Roughly half (51.2%) also identify as "get
things done" pragmatists (C), and 44.2% as curious learners (B).
Aspiring or active contributors (D) account for 31.3%, and strategic
decision-makers (E) for 14.8%. Because the question allows multiple
selections, respondents often combine personas — e.g., enthusiast and
pragmatist. The UpSet chart below the bars makes these combinations explicit:
each column is one exact set of personas, the connected dots show which
personas it contains, and the bar above counts how many respondents chose
exactly that combination. Combinations with fewer than 5 respondents are not
shown.

**Persona definitions** (from the survey prompt):

**A. Love the idea behind Nix or NixOS.** Maybe you spread the word among
friends and coworkers. Interested in or enthusiastic about free and open
source software, Linux, home automation, online communities, or
distro-hopping.

**B. Curious about Nix and how it works.** Maybe you enjoy or want to
learn functional programming, or just want to learn new things. Identify
as: Nix-curious developer, student of a technical field, educator, or
academic researcher.

**C. Use Nix or NixOS to get things done** or boost your team's
productivity, learn it to grow career opportunities, or use it on the job
because someone said so. Identify as: system administrator, employee
developer, DevOps engineer, natural scientist, open source software
author, or early-career professional.

**D. Work or want to work on the Nix ecosystem** rather than just with
it. At least one of: aspiring contributor, novice contributor, drive-by
contributor, package maintainer, code owner, community team member, or
sponsor.

**E. Make strategic decisions** for your team or company: which
technologies to adopt, which skills to train, which projects to support
or invest in, which services or products to offer. Examples: entrepreneur,
team lead, software architect, CTO, sales executive, public service
administrator.

data: D. I (want to) work on Nix
B. I'm curious how Nix works
C. I use Nix to get things done
A. I love the idea behind Nix
A. I love the idea behind Nix
B. I'm curious how Nix works
C. I use Nix to get things done
D. I (want to) work on Nix

title.subtext: 10 combination(s) not shown (below 5 respondents or beyond the display cap).

charts.caption:
**UpSet row key** (short code → full statement):

- **A** — A. I love the idea behind Nix
- **B** — B. I'm curious how Nix works
- **C** — C. I use Nix to get things done
- **D** — D. I (want to) work on Nix
- **E** — E. I'm a decision-maker

rows.title: Self-described skills
rows.question: Which of these describe you?
rows.commentary:
Respondents pick the traits that describe their relationship with Nix
across a 12-item checklist. Tutorial reliance leads at 62.5%, followed
by using the module system for one's own configuration (56.3%) and
having used overlays (53.7%). The share that feels comfortable
contributing upstream (23.3%), reads the Nix source (19.4%), or
claims to understand every error message (4.0%) drops sharply.

data: I completely understand all Nix error messages.
I have read some portion of the Nix source code and understand it.
I understand some deeper aspect of the module system (e.g. submodules, freeform modules, priority system, etc).
I understand a portion of the ecosystem enough to make architectural choices (e.g. one of the language frameworks, stdenv, modules, Nix experimental features, etc).
I feel comfortable enough to contribute upstream.
I usually can write my own Nix expressions without aide.
I can write my own NixOS modules.
I use the module system to create my own NixOS configuration.
I usually rely on blog posts, video tutorials, or examples to help me write expressions.

rows.title: Wished-for improvements
rows.question: You can afford about 3 improvements. Which aspects of the Nix package manager do you pick?
rows.commentary:
Asked to pick about three areas they would improve, respondents
prioritise flakes (50.3%) — the most-cited single area — alongside
error messages (47.6%) and the reference manual (41.5%). Performance
(39.2%) and language usability (31.2%) form the next tier. Platform
expansion (Windows 6.4%, FreeBSD 3.5%) ranks low.

data: Nix as a library (Unstable C++ API, C API, bindings in other languages)

rows.title: Regular toolset
rows.question: Do you consider Nix part of your regular toolset?
rows.commentary:
A clear majority — 81.1% — consider Nix part of their regular toolset.
15.2% do not, and 3.7% skipped the question.

rows.title: Help-search success rate
rows.question: When you look for help with Nix or NixOS, how often do you find a useful or acceptable answer?
rows.commentary:
Respondents largely find what they need: 48.8% say they reach a useful
answer "often" and another 6.7% "always". 30.4% land on "sometimes",
while only a small minority report rare success (4.5%) or none at all
(0.3%).

rows.title: Involvement rate by years using Nix
rows.question: Which of these best describe your involvement with the Nix ecosystem? × How many years have you been using Nix (at any level of involvement)?
rows.commentary:
Among respondents in each experience band, the share reporting each involvement
role. Bands exclude non-users and undisclosed experience.

data: I use Nix to install software
I manage or maintain machines running NixOS
I contribute packages or patches to Nixpkgs
I have merge access to Nixpkgs
I develop tools or infrastructure that use or integrate with Nix, Nixpkgs, or NixOS

rows.title: Involvement lift by years using Nix
rows.question: Which of these best describe your involvement with the Nix ecosystem? × How many years have you been using Nix (at any level of involvement)?
rows.commentary:
How much more (or less) likely each involvement role is within an experience band
relative to the overall baseline (1.0× = no difference). Bands exclude non-users
and undisclosed experience.

data: I use Nix to install software
I manage or maintain machines running NixOS
I contribute packages or patches to Nixpkgs
I have merge access to Nixpkgs
I develop tools or infrastructure that use or integrate with Nix, Nixpkgs, or NixOS

rows.title: Expertise vs Experience
rows.question: How would you describe your skill level with Nix? × How many years have you been using Nix (at any level of involvement)?
rows.commentary:
Each row sums to 100%. Beginners are concentrated in <2 years (88.0%);
Intermediate peaks at 1-2 years (43.3%) and extends through 3-4 years
(28.0%); Advanced spreads from 3 years onward, peaking at 3-4 years
(30.3%). Self-described skill and time-spent aren't strictly aligned —
2.0% of Advanced respondents report <1 year using Nix and 2.3% of Beginners
report 5+ years.

rows.title: Experience vs Expertise
rows.question: How many years have you been using Nix (at any level of involvement)? × How would you describe your skill level with Nix?
rows.commentary:
The complement of the previous chart: each row (a tenure cohort) sums
to 100%, showing how self-described skill distributes within that
cohort. Useful for the dual question — "what does this experience level
look like in skill terms?" rather than "what experience does this skill
level have?"

rows.title: Trait rate by skill level
rows.question: Which of these describe you? × How would you describe your skill level with Nix?
rows.commentary:
Each cell: of respondents at this skill level, what percent picked this trait.
Cells in a column do NOT sum to 100% — traits are multi-choice.

Beginners overwhelmingly rely on tutorials (83%); rate drops to 57% at
Intermediate and 18% at Advanced. Writing own NixOS modules, understanding
overlays, and contributing upstream all climb steeply from under 20% at
Beginner to over 70% at Advanced.

data: I usually rely on blog posts, video tutorials, or examples to help me write expressions.
I usually can write my own Nix expressions without aide.
I use the module system to create my own NixOS configuration.
I can write my own NixOS modules.
I understand some deeper aspect of the module system (e.g. submodules, freeform modules, priority system, etc).
I feel comfortable enough to contribute upstream.
I understand a portion of the ecosystem enough to make architectural choices (e.g. one of the language frameworks, stdenv, modules, Nix experimental features, etc).
I have read some portion of the Nix source code and understand it.
I completely understand all Nix error messages.

rows.title: Trait lift by skill level
rows.question: Which of these describe you? × How would you describe your skill level with Nix?
rows.commentary:
Each cell: how over- or under-represented this skill level is among trait-pickers,
relative to the overall skill distribution. 1.0× = baseline, >1.0× = over-represented,
<1.0× = under-represented. Strips out the population-size imbalance.

Only "rely on tutorials" is over-represented among Beginners (1.30×). Every other
trait skews Advanced. Strongest Advanced lifts: "deeper module aspect" (3.34×),
"contribute upstream" (3.24×), "architectural choices" (2.79×).

data: I usually rely on blog posts, video tutorials, or examples to help me write expressions.
I usually can write my own Nix expressions without aide.
I use the module system to create my own NixOS configuration.
I can write my own NixOS modules.
I understand some deeper aspect of the module system (e.g. submodules, freeform modules, priority system, etc).
I feel comfortable enough to contribute upstream.
I understand a portion of the ecosystem enough to make architectural choices (e.g. one of the language frameworks, stdenv, modules, Nix experimental features, etc).
I have read some portion of the Nix source code and understand it.
I completely understand all Nix error messages.

rows.title: Trait rate by years using Nix
rows.question: Which of these describe you? × How many years have you been using Nix (at any level of involvement)?
rows.commentary:
Each cell: of respondents with this much experience, what percent picked this trait.
Cells in a column do NOT sum to 100% — traits are multi-choice.

Tutorial reliance starts at 76% in the first year and falls to 26% by 11+ years.
Most "advanced" trait rates climb steeply through years 1–6 and largely plateau
after. Group sizes shrink rapidly past 5 years (665 → 225 → 98 → 53 → 42), so
cells in the rightmost columns are noisier.

data: I usually rely on blog posts, video tutorials, or examples to help me write expressions.
I usually can write my own Nix expressions without aide.
I use the module system to create my own NixOS configuration.
I can write my own NixOS modules.
I understand some deeper aspect of the module system (e.g. submodules, freeform modules, priority system, etc).
I feel comfortable enough to contribute upstream.
I understand a portion of the ecosystem enough to make architectural choices (e.g. one of the language frameworks, stdenv, modules, Nix experimental features, etc).
I have read some portion of the Nix source code and understand it.
I completely understand all Nix error messages.

rows.title: Trait lift by years using Nix
rows.question: Which of these describe you? × How many years have you been using Nix (at any level of involvement)?
rows.commentary:
Each cell: how over- or under-represented this experience bucket is among trait-pickers,
relative to the overall experience distribution. 1.0× = baseline, >1.0× = over-represented,
<1.0× = under-represented. Strips out the population-size imbalance.

Tutorial reliance is the only trait over-represented in year-1 respondents
(1.20×). All other traits skew toward more-experienced respondents, with
strongest lifts at 5+ years for "deeper module aspect" (2.1× → 2.4×) and
"contribute upstream" (2.1× → 2.8×). Group sizes shrink rapidly past 5 years,
so cells in the rightmost columns are noisier.

data: I usually rely on blog posts, video tutorials, or examples to help me write expressions.
I usually can write my own Nix expressions without aide.
I use the module system to create my own NixOS configuration.
I can write my own NixOS modules.
I understand some deeper aspect of the module system (e.g. submodules, freeform modules, priority system, etc).
I feel comfortable enough to contribute upstream.

rows.title: Workplace adoption
rows.question: Does your workplace use Nix?
rows.commentary:
Among respondents whose free-text answers could be normalised, 12.8%
reported their workplace uses Nix and 29.2% said it does not. A
majority — 57.9% — gave answers that fell into the "Other" category.

Free-text answers normalised to Yes / No / Other.

rows.title: Workplace decision-maker
rows.question: Are you the one that made that decision?
rows.commentary:
Of respondents in a Nix-using workplace, 15.7% report having made the
adoption decision themselves; 23.9% did not. The largest share
(60.4%) again falls into "Other".

Free-text answers normalised to Yes / No / Other.

rows.title: Contributor experience
rows.question: If you’ve contributed to Nixpkgs or are interested in contributing, which of the following describe your experience or situation?
rows.commentary:
Onboarding is the dominant theme. 35.8% plan to contribute in the future, and
another 36% want to contribute now but don't know how to get started or how
to join existing efforts. Roughly 31% have actually contributed (combining "on
my own" and "with community help"). Feedback friction shows up at modest rates:
7.0% report slow feedback, 5.8% got stuck after starting, 2.1% found received
feedback unhelpful. Employer policy is rarely a blocker (0.6%). The UpSet
chart shows which of these experiences co-occur for the same respondent —
each column is one exact combination of selected statements, sized by the bar
above. Only the most common combinations are shown; rarer ones (and any with
fewer than 5 respondents) are omitted and counted in the chart's caption.

data: I can’t contribute because my employer doesn’t allow it.
I received feedback, but it was unhelpful or unclear.
I tried to contribute, but got stuck after I started.
It took too long to receive feedback.
Everything I wanted to work on was already being handled by others and I don't feel the need to get involved.
I want to contribute to an existing effort, but don’t know how to join in.
I was able to contribute with help from the community.
I was able to contribute on my own.
I would like to contribute, but I don’t know how to get started.
I plan to contribute in the future.
I would like to contribute, but I don’t know how to get started.
I want to contribute to an existing effort, but don’t know how to join in.
I tried to contribute, but got stuck after I started.
I received feedback, but it was unhelpful or unclear.
It took too long to receive feedback.
I can’t contribute because my employer doesn’t allow it.
Everything I wanted to work on was already being handled by others and I don't feel the need to get involved.
I was able to contribute on my own.

title.subtext: 132 combination(s) not shown (below 5 respondents or beyond the display cap).

charts.caption:
**UpSet row key** (short code → full statement):

- **No plan** — I don’t plan to contribute.
- **Start?** — I would like to contribute, but I don’t know how to get started.
- **Join?** — I want to contribute to an existing effort, but don’t know how to join in.
- **Stuck** — I tried to contribute, but got stuck after I started.
- **Unclear** — I received feedback, but it was unhelpful or unclear.
- **Slow** — It took too long to receive feedback.
- **Blocked** — I can’t contribute because my employer doesn’t allow it.
- **Covered** — Everything I wanted to work on was already being handled by others and I don't feel the need to get involved.
- **Solo** — I was able to contribute on my own.
- **Helped** — I was able to contribute with help from the community.
- **Future** — I plan to contribute in the future.

rows.title: Donation incentives
rows.question: What would encourage you to donate to the NixOS Foundation?
rows.commentary:
Transparency is the top motivator: 26.7% would be encouraged by better
financial and operational reports — more than any other option. Tax
deductibility (16.1%), a maintained LTS release (15.3%), official support or
services (12.9%), and donor perks (12.4%) cluster as secondary motivators.
Ear-marking donations to specific areas lands last at 5.6%.

data: Donor perks (e.g. recognition on website, Discourse badges, swag, exclusive access)
The ability to deduct donations from tax
Better insight (e.g. more regular and detailed financial and operational reports)

rows.title: Foundation funding priorities
rows.question: Which areas should the NixOS Foundation prioritise excess funds for?
rows.commentary:
Each bar shows how respondents distributed their ranks across positions, with the
remainder unranked. Documentation is placed first far more often than any other
area. Nixpkgs package maintenance and security follow closely. Community health,
NixOS releases, and Nixpkgs architecture cluster in the middle. Marketing and
community events are the priorities most often ranked low or left unranked.

data: Deployment systems (e.g. Nixops or a replacement)
CI systems (e.g. Hydra or a replacement)
Official infrastructure (e.g. faster builds and caches)
Improved Nix/NixOS support (e.g. hardware, platforms, installers)

rows.title: Objects you interact with
rows.question: Which objects do you interact with or search for most often across the ecosystem?
rows.commentary:
Each bar shows how respondents distributed their ranks across grouped positions
(1-3, 4-6, 7-10), with the remainder left unranked. Search objects dominate the
top band: package search and NixOS option search are placed in the top three far
more often than anything else. Configuration examples, manuals, the command-line
interface, and development environments form the middle tier. Peripheral
objects — job postings, press information, reviews, paid support — are mostly
left unranked.

data: NixOS option search (option names, option documentation)

rows.title: Help resources
rows.question: When you're looking for help with Nix or NixOS, which resources do you turn to most often? (Rank your top 5. If you usually web search first, pick the resources you click on most.)
rows.commentary:
Each bar shows the share of respondents who placed a resource at each of their
top five positions, with the remainder unranked. The official NixOS Wiki and the
reference manuals are placed in the top five most often, followed by GitHub
issues and discussions. LLMs already appear in many top-five lists, ahead of
nix.dev. Social channels — Reddit, Stack Overflow, Matrix, Discord — trail the
official and code-adjacent sources; Mastodon and Twitter/X are rarely chosen for
help-seeking.
